items.correct: An undifferentiated cell capable of dividing to produce more stem cells or differentiating into specialised cell types.
items.misconception: Stem cells are taken only from embryos and cannot be found in adults.
items.random_wrong: Stem cells are simply cells that haven't grown big enough yet.
items.why: Media coverage of embryonic stem cells overshadows adult stem cells, so students think 'stem cell' = 'embryo cell'.

items.q: What is the main role of mitochondria in a cell?
items.correct: To carry out aerobic respiration, producing ATP that cells use as an energy currency.
items.misconception: Mitochondria produce energy by creating it from nothing.
items.random_wrong: Mitochondria store sunlight and release it for photosynthesis.
items.why: The phrase 'powerhouse of the cell' makes students think energy is created by mitochondria, missing that they transfer energy from glucose to ATP.

items.q: What is the difference between a silent and a missense mutation?
items.correct: A silent mutation changes a base but the codon still codes for the same amino acid; a missense mutation changes the amino acid encoded.
items.misconception: A silent mutation has no effect because no base actually changes.
items.random_wrong: Missense and silent mutations always produce a non-functional protein.
items.why: Students think 'silent' means 'no change at the DNA level' rather than 'no change at the protein level' (due to genetic code degeneracy).
Correct answer: Vertebrates. “Invertebrates” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Dolphin. “Frog” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If there are 5 vertebrate groups and 8 animals per group, total animals?
items.why: The real figure is 40. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
